title: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis

items.subtopic: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis
items.title: Differentiating Upper vs. Lower Motor Neuron Lesions in Weakness
items.content: Upper motor neuron lesions lead to specific patterns of muscle weakness, increased tone (spasticity), hyperreflexia, and the presence of a Babinski sign, while lower motor neuron lesions result in flaccid paralysis, decreased or absent reflexes, atrophy, and fasciculations.
keyPoints: Weakness from upper motor neurons affects extensors and abductors of the upper limb and flexors of the lower limb; spasticity is velocity-dependent with a 'clasp-knife' release.
Lower motor neuron weakness involves only muscles supplied by the affected structure, leading to flaccidity or normal tone, decreased reflexes, atrophy, and fasciculations.
Hyperreflexia in upper motor neuron lesions may be accompanied by loss of cutaneous reflexes and a Babinski sign; lower motor neuron lesions depress muscle stretch reflexes.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis

items.subtopic: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis
items.title: Identifying Myopathic vs. Neuropathic Weakness
items.content: Myopathic weakness typically presents as symmetric proximal muscle weakness without sensory deficits, while neuropathic weakness often manifests as distal weakness with possible early atrophy and fasciculations.
keyPoints: Symmetric proximal weakness suggests myopathy; distal weakness is more likely to be neuropathic.
Fasciculations and early atrophy are indicative of neuropathic (lower motor neuron) weakness.
Myopathic weakness does not follow a specific pattern, unlike the characteristic patterns seen in upper or lower motor neuron lesions.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis

items.subtopic: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis
items.title: Understanding Muscle Tone Abnormalities
items.content: Increased muscle tone can manifest as spasticity, rigidity, or paratonia, each associated with different underlying neurological conditions.
keyPoints: Spasticity is velocity-dependent, affects antigravity muscles, and shows a 'clasp-knife' phenomenon in upper motor neuron diseases.
Rigidity presents as uniform hypertonia throughout the range of motion, often with cogwheel quality, seen in extrapyramidal disorders like Parkinson's disease.
Paratonia varies irregularly with relaxation degree, affects flexors and extensors equally, and is linked to frontal lobe disease.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis

items.title: Upper Motor Neuron Weakness: Distal vs. Proximal Muscle Involvement
items.content: Lesions of upper motor neurons or their descending axons lead to decreased activation of lower motor neurons, causing weakness more pronounced in distal muscle groups compared to proximal ones. Axial movements are generally spared unless the lesion is severe and bilateral.
keyPoints: Distal muscles affected more than proximal
Axial movements typically spared
Spasticity common but may not be acute
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Lower Motor Neuron Weakness: Mechanisms and EMG Findings
items.content: Weakness arises from a decrease in the number of muscle fibers that can be activated due to loss or dysfunction of $\alpha$ motor neurons. Loss of $\gamma$ motor neurons decreases muscle tone but not weakness. EMG shows diminished maximal discharge frequency, fasciculations, and fibrillation potentials.
keyPoints: Decrease in muscle fiber activation
$\alpha$ motor neuron loss causes weakness
Absent stretch reflex suggests spindle afferent involvement
EMG: diminished discharge frequency, fasciculations, fibrillation potentials
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Neuromuscular Junction Weakness: Characteristics and Fatigability
items.content: Weakness in neuromuscular junction disorders varies with muscle rest state. Strength declines with sustained or repeated contractions, indicative of fatigable weakness as seen in myasthenia gravis.
keyPoints: Variable degree and distribution of weakness
Strength influenced by preceding activity
Fatigable weakness suggests neuromuscular junction disorders
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Hemiparesis: Localization and Etiology
items.content: Hemiparesis results from an upper motor neuron lesion above the midcervical spinal cord, often due to vascular etiologies or inflammatory processes. The presence of additional neurological deficits helps localize the lesion, with language disorders suggesting a cortical origin and homonymous visual field defects indicating either cortical or subcortical lesions.
keyPoints: Hemiparesis is caused by an upper motor neuron lesion above the midcervical spinal cord.
Language disorders point to a cortical lesion; homonymous visual field defects suggest cortical or subcortical hemispheric lesions.
Acute hemiparesis often results from vascular etiologies, rapidly expanding lesions, or inflammatory processes.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · DISTRIBUTION OF WEAKNESS (part 1)

items.title: Investigation of Hemiparesis
items.content: For acute hemiparesis, initial investigations include a CT scan of the brain and laboratory studies. If the CT is normal or in subacute/chronic cases, MRI of the brain is recommended to identify potential causes such as neoplasms or vascular malformations.
keyPoints: Acute hemiparesis investigation starts with a CT scan of the brain and lab studies.
If CT is normal or in subacute/chronic cases, proceed with MRI of the brain.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · DISTRIBUTION OF WEAKNESS (part 1)

items.title: Acute Paraparesis: Causes and Imaging
items.content: Acute paraparesis is most commonly caused by intraspinal lesions. Initial imaging with MRI can reveal compressive lesions, infarction, or vascular anomalies. Hemispheric causes include anterior cerebral artery ischemia and acute hydrocephalus.
keyPoints: Acute paraparesis often results from an intraspinal lesion.
MRI may show compressive lesions, infarction, or vascular anomalies.
Hemispheric causes include anterior cerebral artery ischemia and acute hydrocephalus.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · DISTRIBUTION OF WEAKNESS (part 1)

items.title: Approach to Quadriparesis
items.content: Quadriparesis evaluation depends on acuity and alertness. Acute quadriparesis in obtunded patients starts with a CT scan of the brain, while acute upper motor neuron signs in alert patients prompt an MRI of the cervical cord. Chronic quadriparesis requires distinguishing between upper and lower motor neuron diseases through clinical assessment and targeted imaging.
keyPoints: Acute quadriparesis in obtunded patients: start with CT scan of the brain.
Acute upper motor neuron signs in alert patients: initial test is MRI of the cervical cord.
Chronic quadriparesis: differentiate between upper and lower motor neuron diseases clinically.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · DISTRIBUTION OF WEAKNESS (part 2)

items.title: Diagnosis of Acute Monoparesis
items.content: Acute monoparesis evaluation varies by type. Distal upper motor neuron weakness without sensory impairment suggests focal cortical ischemia, akin to acute hemiparesis. Lower motor neuron weakness with sensory loss or pain indicates a peripheral cause and begins with EMG and nerve conduction studies.
keyPoints: Distal upper motor neuron monoparesis without sensory impairment: consider focal cortical ischemia.
Lower motor neuron monoparesis with sensory symptoms: likely peripheral nerve, root, or plexus involvement.
Evaluation for lower motor neuron weakness begins with EMG and nerve conduction studies.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · DISTRIBUTION OF WEAKNESS (part 2)

items.title: Identifying Proximal Weakness
items.content: Proximal weakness evaluation involves distinguishing between myopathy, neuromuscular junction diseases, and anterior horn cell disease. Myopathies cause symmetric pelvic or shoulder girdle muscle weakness; neuromuscular junction disorders like myasthenia gravis present with fluctuating symptoms including ptosis and diplopia. Anterior horn cell disease shows asymmetric proximal weakness without numbness.
keyPoints: Myopathy: causes symmetric weakness of pelvic or shoulder girdle muscles.
Neuromuscular junction diseases (e.g., myasthenia gravis): present with fluctuating symptoms and may include ptosis, diplopia.
Anterior horn cell disease: typically shows asymmetric proximal weakness without numbness.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · DISTRIBUTION OF WEAKNESS (part 2)

items.subtopic: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis
items.question: Which term refers to severe weakness where a muscle cannot be contracted at all?
items.explanation: Paralysis indicates weakness so severe that a muscle cannot be contracted at all.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis

items.subtopic: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis
items.question: What type of hypertonia is characterized by a 'clasp-knife' phenomenon?
items.explanation: Spasticity, associated with upper motor neuron disease, shows the 'clasp-knife' phenomenon.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis

items.subtopic: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis
items.question: Which condition is likely to show symmetric proximal weakness?
items.explanation: Symmetric proximal weakness is typically indicative of myopathic weakness.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis

items.subtopic: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis
items.question: What is the term for visible or palpable twitches within a muscle due to spontaneous discharge?
items.explanation: Fasciculations are visible or palpable twitches within a muscle caused by the spontaneous discharge of a motor unit.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis

items.question: Which type of motor neuron lesion typically results in spasticity and affects distal muscles more severely than proximal ones?
items.explanation: Upper motor neuron lesions produce weakness with spasticity and more severe effects on distal muscles.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · PATHOGENESIS

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of bilateral corticobulbar lesions?
options: Decreased maximal discharge frequency on EMG
items.explanation: Bilateral corticobulbar lesions produce pseudobulbar palsy, which includes dysarthria and emotional lability.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · PATHOGENESIS

items.question: What is the effect of lower motor neuron weakness on muscle tone?
options: Increased muscle tone due to spasticity
Decreased muscle tone due to loss of gamma motor neurons
Variable changes depending on the specific muscles involved
items.explanation: Lower motor neuron weakness can lead to decreased muscle tone, especially when gamma motor neurons are affected.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · PATHOGENESIS

items.question: In myasthenia gravis, what is the pattern of muscle strength during sustained contractions?
options: Strength remains constant regardless of activity
items.explanation: In myasthenia gravis, muscle strength declines during sustained contractions due to neuromuscular junction dysfunction.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which of the following is a common cause of acute quadriparesis with onset over minutes?
items.explanation: Acute quadriparesis with onset over minutes can result from disorders of upper motor neurons or muscle, including electrolyte disturbances.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · DISTRIBUTION OF WEAKNESS (part 1)

items.question: What type of hemiparesis suggests a lesion in the high cervical spinal cord?
options: Hemiparesis with ipsilateral cranial nerve signs
Pure motor hemiparesis without facial weakness
items.explanation: The absence of cranial nerve signs or facial weakness suggests a lesion in the high cervical spinal cord, especially if associated with Brown-Séquard syndrome.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · DISTRIBUTION OF WEAKNESS (part 1)

items.question: Which imaging study is typically first performed for acute hemiparesis?
items.explanation: Investigation of acute hemiparesis usually starts with a CT scan of the brain.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · DISTRIBUTION OF WEAKNESS (part 1)

items.question: What is a common cause of subacute paraparesis?
items.explanation: Subacute or chronic hemiparesis may be caused by peripheral neuropathy, such as Guillain-Barré syndrome.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · DISTRIBUTION OF WEAKNESS (part 1)

items.question: In a patient presenting with acute quadriparesis and alertness, which initial test is most appropriate?
options: Blood studies for muscle enzymes and electrolytes
items.explanation: If quadriparesis develops acutely in an alert patient, MRI of the cervical cord is usually the initial test.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · DISTRIBUTION OF WEAKNESS (part 2)

items.question: Which condition is most likely if a patient presents with acute monoparesis predominantly distal and without sensory impairment?
items.explanation: Acute monoparesis that is predominantly distal and of upper motor neuron type without sensory impairment suggests focal cortical ischemia.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · DISTRIBUTION OF WEAKNESS (part 2)

items.question: In subacute or chronic monoparesis, what is the likely origin if weakness and atrophy develop over weeks to months?
items.explanation: Weakness and atrophy developing over weeks or months are usually of lower motor neuron origin.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · DISTRIBUTION OF WEAKNESS (part 2)

items.question: What is the initial evaluation step for a patient with proximal weakness likely due to myopathy?
items.explanation: For proximal weakness likely due to myopathy, evaluation begins with determining the serum creatine kinase level.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · DISTRIBUTION OF WEAKNESS (part 2)

items.subtopic: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis
items.statement: Increased tone that varies irregularly with relaxation is known as spasticity.
items.explanation: This description fits paratonia, not spasticity.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis

items.subtopic: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis
items.statement: Muscle stretch reflexes are usually increased with lower motor neuron lesions.
items.explanation: Muscle stretch reflexes are generally depressed with lower motor neuron lesions.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis

items.statement: Spontaneous discharges in muscle fibers are seen with upper motor neuron lesions.
items.explanation: Spontaneous discharges occur with lower motor neuron lesions, not upper.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: Fatigable weakness is characteristic of myopathic disorders.
items.explanation: Fatigable weakness suggests neuromuscular junction disorders like myasthenia gravis, not typically myopathies.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: Crossed paralyses consist of ipsilateral cranial nerve signs and contralateral hemiparesis.
items.explanation: Some brainstem lesions produce crossed paralyses, consisting of ipsilateral cranial nerve signs and contralateral hemiparesis.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · DISTRIBUTION OF WEAKNESS (part 1)

items.statement: Chronic hemiparesis is most commonly due to rapidly expanding lesions.
items.explanation: Chronic hemiparesis usually results from a neoplasm, vascular malformation, chronic subdural hematoma, or degenerative disease.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · DISTRIBUTION OF WEAKNESS (part 1)

items.statement: Acute distal lower-limb weakness can result from acute toxic polyneuropathy.
items.explanation: Acute distal lower-limb weakness can occasionally result from an acute toxic polyneuropathy.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · DISTRIBUTION OF WEAKNESS (part 2)

items.statement: Anterior horn cell disease typically presents with symmetric proximal weakness.
items.explanation: In anterior horn cell disease, proximal weakness is usually asymmetric.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · DISTRIBUTION OF WEAKNESS (part 2)

items.subtopic: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis
items.assertion: Weakness from impaired neuromuscular transmission has a specific pattern of involvement.
items.reason: Impaired neuromuscular transmission does not have a specific pattern of muscle weakness.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the reason correctly states that there's no specific pattern.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis

items.subtopic: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis
items.assertion: Spasticity affects both flexors and extensors equally.
items.reason: Spasticity predominantly affects antigravity muscles, such as upper-limb flexors and lower-limb extensors.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the reason correctly describes spasticity's effect on specific muscle groups.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis

items.assertion: Upper motor neuron lesions result in decreased maximal discharge frequency of motor units.
items.reason: Weakness from upper motor neuron lesions is due to reduced activation of lower motor neurons.
items.explanation: Decreased maximal discharge frequency is characteristic of lower, not upper, motor neuron weakness.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · PATHOGENESIS

items.assertion: Psychogenic weakness can be identified by the contraction of antagonists during supposed agonist muscle activation.
items.reason: This pattern indicates inconsistency and lack of a neuroanatomic basis for the weakness.
items.explanation: Both assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · PATHOGENESIS

items.assertion: Acute paraparesis is most commonly caused by an intraspinal lesion.
items.reason: The spinal cord origin may not be recognized initially if the legs are flaccid and areflexic.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the spinal cord origin might not be immediately apparent.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · DISTRIBUTION OF WEAKNESS (part 1)

items.assertion: Generalized weakness from CNS disorders is usually associated with normal mental function.
items.reason: CNS disorders typically cause changes in consciousness or cognition along with spasticity and hyperreflexia.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because generalized weakness from CNS disorders is not associated with normal mental function; it usually involves changes in consciousness or cognition.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · DISTRIBUTION OF WEAKNESS (part 1)

items.assertion: Distal symmetric weakness over time is due to peripheral neuropathy.
items.reason: Peripheral neuropathy often presents with distal symmetric weakness and numbness.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · DISTRIBUTION OF WEAKNESS (part 2)

items.reason: Myopathy often produces symmetric weakness of the pelvic or shoulder girdle muscles.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: 26 Neurologic Causes of Weakness and Paralysis · DISTRIBUTION OF WEAKNESS (part 2)
